description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who is trying to quickly extract the most relevant information from a login audit log in order to hand it off to a security analyst.

The raw log already exists at:
/home/user/logs/app.log

It contains **exactly three** lines, each in this canonical format (all on one line, no trailing spaces):

YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS,ms [INFO] [user-service] User '<username>' logged in from <ip> (session:<session-id>)

Your assignment:

1. Read /home/user/logs/app.log.
2. Using only classic column-manipulation utilities that a shell provides (for example: cut, paste, awk, etc.), extract four specific pieces of information from every log line in the exact sequence shown below:
   • the entire timestamp field (that is “YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS,ms”)  
   • the username without the surrounding single quotes  
   • the source IP address  
   • the session-id without the literal text “session:” and without the parentheses
3. Assemble those four components into **one new file**:
/home/user/logs/login_summary.log

The output file must:

• Contain **exactly four lines**.  
  – Line 1 is a header that must read literally:
    timestamp|user|ip|session  
  – Lines 2-4 each correspond to one record from the source log and must follow this rigid template (no spaces except the single literal space in the timestamp portion, no trailing spaces, fields separated by the pipe character “|”):

YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS,ms|<username>|<ip>|<session-id>

• Preserve the original chronological order found in /home/user/logs/app.log.
• Use UNIX line endings (LF).

Example of the correct look (values will be different, header must match exactly):

timestamp|user|ip|session
2023-07-21 10:33:45,123|alice|10.0.0.12|abc123
…

When finished, you should be able to:

cat /home/user/logs/login_summary.log  

and see the header plus three properly-formatted data lines.

Important notes for the automated checker:

• The file /home/user/logs/login_summary.log must exist.  
• It must have exactly 4 lines.  
• Each line must match the pattern described above, including the header text and pipe separators.  
• No extra blank lines or spaces are allowed—content must be byte-for-byte identical to the expected result.

Feel free to display whatever intermediate console output you like, but only the final state of /home/user/logs/login_summary.log will be graded.
truth:
Initial state before the agent runs:
-----------------------------------
File: /home/user/logs/app.log
Exact content (three lines, LF line endings):

2023-07-21 10:33:45,123 [INFO] [user-service] User 'alice' logged in from 10.0.0.12 (session:abc123)
2023-07-21 11:12:07,456 [INFO] [user-service] User 'bob' logged in from 10.0.0.15 (session:def456)
2023-07-21 12:55:22,789 [INFO] [user-service] User 'charlie' logged in from 10.0.0.18 (session:ghi789)

Directory /home/user/logs already exists and is writable by the current user.

Expected state after successful completion:
-------------------------------------------
File: /home/user/logs/login_summary.log
Exact content (four lines, LF line endings, no trailing spaces):

timestamp|user|ip|session
2023-07-21 10:33:45,123|alice|10.0.0.12|abc123
2023-07-21 11:12:07,456|bob|10.0.0.15|def456
2023-07-21 12:55:22,789|charlie|10.0.0.18|ghi789

Permissions: readable and writable by the current user (0600 or 0644 both acceptable).

The automated test will:
1. Verify that /home/user/logs/login_summary.log exists.
2. Read its bytes and compare them exactly to the four lines above.
3. Ensure there are exactly four newline characters (i.e., four lines, no extra blank at end).

No other files are inspected or required.
